title: St. Matthias the Apostle, St. Agapetus of Rome, St. Lucian of Antioch & St. Phib the Hermit
narrative:
St. Matthias the Apostle, St. Agapetus the Martyr, St. Lucian of Antioch, and St. Phib the Hermit of Egypt

On the twenty-fourth of Yekatit, the holy Church gathers four witnesses whose lives illuminate the breadth of the apostolic and scholarly tradition, from the man chosen by lot to fill the vacancy among the Twelve to the young martyr of Rome, from the great biblical scholar of Antioch to the solitary of the Egyptian desert. Saint Matthias the Apostle stands first in this assembly: chosen not by any human authority but by the falling of the lot — that ancient biblical mechanism by which the community acknowledged that the choice ultimately belonged to God — Matthias was incorporated into the Twelve as a full apostle, eyewitness of the entire earthly ministry of the Lord from the baptism of John to the Ascension, and bearer of the apostolic commission to preach, baptise, and heal in the Name of Christ. The tradition sends him to Ethiopia itself in some accounts, making him a figure of particular closeness to the Ethiopian church's own sense of apostolic origin.

Saint Agapetus of Rome is the young martyr whom the Ethiopian tradition commemorates as one of the fresh-faced witnesses of the early Roman persecutions — a youth of fifteen who was brought before the prefect Antiochus and refused, with a composure beyond his years, to offer sacrifice to the gods. The prefect, perhaps startled by his serenity, subjected him to extended torture over several days before finally executing him, and the tradition records that Agapetus bore each stage of his suffering with a joy that the witnesses found inexplicable and disturbing. The early martyrologies frequently note this quality — the joy of the young martyr that makes the executioner uneasy — as a sign of the presence of the Spirit, who gives in that hour what earthly courage alone could never produce.

Saint Lucian of Antioch is the great discovery of this day: one of the most significant biblical scholars of the early Church, a presbyter of Antioch who produced a revised edition of the Greek New Testament and the Septuagint that became the standard text of the eastern Mediterranean world and the basis of the Byzantine ecclesiastical text. He founded a theological school in Antioch that trained some of the most important figures of the fourth century, including Arius himself — a detail the tradition preserves without embarrassment, noting that the teacher cannot always be held responsible for the student's deviations. Lucian died a martyr in 312 under Maximinus Daia, and his death was the final seal upon a life that had served the Word in the most literal sense: giving the Church an accurate and accessible text of the Scriptures.

Abba Phib the Hermit of Egypt is the figure of pure contemplative witness that grounds this assembly in the silence of the desert. Almost nothing is known of Phib beyond his name, his dwelling place in the Egyptian desert, and a few sayings preserved in the Apophthegmata tradition. One of these sayings, however, has been treasured across the centuries: asked what prayer was, Phib replied that prayer was giving God your full attention for as long as you could manage, and then asking His forgiveness for failing to manage it longer. This definition — so simple, so honest about human limitation, so free from the pretension that often attaches to discussions of advanced prayer — captures something essential about the desert tradition's understanding of the contemplative life: not a technique or an achievement but a relationship, characterised above all by the willingness to keep showing up. Matthias chosen by God, Agapetus joyful in torment, Lucian serving the Word, Phib giving his attention: four images of the unreserved response to the divine call. Amen.
